elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a multi-impurity method for the bond-weighted tensor renormalization group (BWTRG) to compute the higher-order moment of physical quantities in a two-dimensional system.
elements.sent-2411-002: The replacement of the bond weight with an impurity matrix in a bond-weighted triad tensor network represents a physical quantity such as the magnetization and the energy.
elements.sent-2411-003: We demonstrate that the accuracy of the proposed method is much higher than the conventional tensor renormalization group for the Ising model and the 5-state Potts model.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, we perform the finite-size scaling analysis and observe that the dimensionless quantity characterizing the structure of the fixed point tensor satisfies the same scaling relation in the critical region as the Binder parameter.
elements.sent-2411-005: The estimated critical temperature dependence on the bond dimension indicates that the exponent relating the correlation length to the bond dimension varies continuously with respect to the BWTRG hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-006: We find that BWTRG with the optimal hyperparameter is more efficient in terms of computational time than alternative approaches based on the matrix product state in estimating the critical temperature.
elements.sent-2411-007: Tensor networks effectively represent the partition function of a classical statistical system or the path integral of a lattice gauge theory.
elements.sent-2411-008: They are also used to represent the wave function of a quantum many-body system.
elements.sent-2411-009: The exact contraction of a tensor network is generally intractable because of the exponential growth of the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-010: The tensor renormalization group (TRG) method approximates the contraction by combining the real-space renormalization group concept and information compression by the truncated singular value decomposition (SVD)~\cite{levin2007tensor .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Variants of TRG, including the higher-order TRG (HOTRG)~\cite{xie2012coarsegraining , have been proposed, and their accuracy and computational efficiency have been improved~\cite{xie2009second,evenbly2015tensor,yang2017loop,hauru2018renormalization,harada2018entanglement,adachi2020anisotropic,kadoh2019renormalization,morita2021global,kadoh2022triad,homma2023nuclear .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One of recent advances is the bond-weighted tensor renormalization group (BWTRG)~\cite{adachi2022bondweighted .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Tuning the bond weight improves the convergence to the fixed point tensor and enhances the accuracy without degrading the computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-014: BWTRG have been extended to the Grassmann tensor network for fermions~\cite{akiyama2022bondweighting and applied to the lattice gauge theory~\cite{akiyama2024tensorb .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: To understand physical properties in a system of interest, it is essential to calculate basic physical quantities such as energy and magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-016: Representative methods for calculating physical quantities based on TRG are numerical differentiation, automatic differentiation, and the introduction of impurities.
elements.sent-2411-017: The impurity method represents a physical quantity by changing some tensors in the tensor network.
elements.sent-2411-018: This method is effective when a drastic change occurs near the critical point or the singular values degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-019: We proposed a method to calculate higher-order moments by coarse-graining a tensor network with multiple impurities~\cite{morita2019calculation .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, since this method is based on HOTRG, the high computational cost limits the bond dimension that could be handled.
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, the TRG-based method required special care to prevent the tensor corresponding to impurities from spreading through the tensor decomposition operation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, no TRG-based method has been proposed to calculate higher-order moments using the impurity tensor.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we propose a method to calculate higher-order moments based on BWTRG.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our idea is to introduce impurities by replacing the bond weight instead of the tensor.
elements.sent-2411-025: This method has lower computational cost than the method based on HOTRG, and it is possible to perform calculations with a larger bond dimension.
elements.sent-2411-026: It has been reported that BWTRG has higher accuracy than TRG and HOTRG even with the same bond dimension.
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, it is expected that the accuracy will be improved in the analysis of phase transitions and critical phenomena using this method.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the next section, we overview BWTRG and explain our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-029: To reduce the computational cost, we reformulate BWTRG with a triad tensor network.
elements.sent-2411-030: We also give the tensor network representation of the partition function and physical quantities in the Ising model and the Potts model.
elements.sent-2411-031: In Sec.~\ref{sec:results , we show the results of numerical calculations and evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The impurity method is suitable for finite-size scaling analysis, as it allows for the calculation of the temperature dependence of physical quantities in finite systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: Compared to the transfer matrix method, which is widely used in tensor network approaches, finite-size scaling offers greater flexibility in analyzing higher-dimensional systems, disordered systems, and a broader range of physical quantities.
elements.sent-2411-034: We demonstrate that combining our method with finite-size scaling analysis enables the precise determination of the critical temperature and critical exponents.
elements.sent-2411-035: We investigate how the error in the critical temperature depends on the bond dimension and observe that the proposed method is the most computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-036: The last section is devoted to discussion and conclusion.

elements.sent-2411-041: TRG and its variants are developed to approximate the contraction by coarse-graining the tensor network.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this paper, we consider a bond-weighted tensor network on the truncated square lattice depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice .}
elements.sent-2411-043: We note that any two-dimensional periodic system can be transformed into this form.
elements.sent-2411-044: A three-index tensor on each vertex of a small square (plaquette) is denoted by $A_i$ ($i=1, \dots, 4$), which is labeled according to the orientation.
elements.sent-2411-045: We assume that $A_i$ is isometric such as \begin{equation}\includegraphics{isometry.pdf}, \label{eq:isometry_A}\end{equation} in the graphical representation.
elements.sent-2411-046: Each bond has a bond weight depicted by a circle, which is assumed to be a diagonal non-negative matrix.
elements.sent-2411-047: The bond weights can be classified into two types.
elements.sent-2411-048: One is on a edge of a plaquette with $A_i$ at the vertices and the other is on a bond connecting those plaquettes.
elements.sent-2411-049: We call the former an inner weight $\sigma_j$ and the latter an outer weight $\tau_j$ ($j=1, 2$).
elements.sent-2411-050: A unit cell of this tensor network network can have four inner weights in general, but only two is independent ones remain after a renormalization step as will become clear later.

elements.sent-2411-052: Avoiding the creation of the four-leg tensor reduces the computational cost of BWTRG as well as TRG~\cite{morita2018tensor .}

elements.sent-2411-065: The dimensional analysis of the fixed point tensor also supports it.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this paper, we will fix the hyperparameter $k$ to this optimal value if not mentioned.
elements.sent-2411-067: The impurity method of tensor networks is a technique for calculating the expected value of a physical quantity, \begin{equation}\langle O \rangle = \frac{\sum_{\{S_x\}} O(\{S_x\}) e^{-H(\{S_x\})/T}}{Z}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: If the physical quantity $O$ is local, the numerator is represented by the tensor network where some of tensors are replaced with others.
elements.sent-2411-069: The contraction of this tensor network with impurities is approximated in a similar manner to the partition function.
elements.sent-2411-070: To calculate the spacial average of a local quantity or its higher-order moments, we proposed the multi-impurity method for HOTRG, where impurity tensors are updated by the systematic summation technique~\cite{morita2019calculation .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Our main proposal in this paper is that we replace some of the bond weights with matrices to represent impurities.
elements.sent-2411-072: The replaced matrix needs not to be diagonal unlike the bond weight.
elements.sent-2411-073: There are two types of the impurity matrix as well as the bond weight, inner and outer.
elements.sent-2411-074: We call the matrix placed at the position of the inner (outer) weight the inner (outer) impurity matrix, which is denoted by $S_j$ ($T_j$).
elements.sent-2411-075: The update rule for the impurity matrix is similar to that for the bond weight.
elements.sent-2411-076: The inner impurity matrix $\tilde{S _j$ in the next step is obtained from the current outer impurity matrix $T_j$ as \begin{equation}\tilde{S}_j = \tau_j^{-k'} T_j \tau_j^{-k'},\end{equation} which corresponds to Eq.~\eqref{eq:update_sigma .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: For the update rule for the outer impurity matrix $\tilde{T _1$ between $\tilde{A _1$ and $\tilde{A _3$, we define a multilinear map $R(M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4)$ as the diagram shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:update-imp .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-079: Depending on the position of impurities, we replace $\sigma_j$ to the inner impurity matrix $S_j$.

elements.sent-2411-214: In this paper, we have proposed a method to calculate physical quantities using impurities in the BWTRG method.
elements.sent-2411-215: We have reformulated BWTRG on the truncated square lattice to reduce its computational cost to $O(\chi^5)$.
elements.sent-2411-216: The key idea of our method is to replace the bond weight with a matrix including impurities.
elements.sent-2411-217: The isometric tensor, which truncates unimportant information, is determined by the singular value decomposition of a part of the tensor network without impurities and made common for all physical quantities, which allows us to handle multiple impurities.
elements.sent-2411-218: We have applied our method to the Ising model and the 5-state Potts model, and demonstrated its effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-219: In particular, the BWTRG method gives more accurate results than the TRG method, and the error is minimized near the optimal hyperparameter $k=-1/2$.
